system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 18: Blues Progressions

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 18 — Blues Progressions

_Pages 93-97._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Blues centrality

> Blues progressions form the basis for so much music now in existence that a knowledge of them if
> very important to the well-rounded musician.

**Significance (p. 93).** Justifies the chapter's importance.

## 12-bar definition

> in this book, it refers to any 12 bar (measure)
> Progression.

**Significance (p. 93).** Defines 'blues' as a 12-bar form for the book.

## Top-note attention rule

> When choosing the inversions, pay particular attention to the melody note (the highest pitch — the
> top note) in each chord.

**Significance (p. 93).** Inversion selection rule based on melody line.

## Smooth voice leading paramount

> Actually, you should
> be watching for smooth voice leading (as was discussed in section 13) wherever possible.

**Significance (p. 93).** Reinforces voice-leading priority in blues.

## Fingers required for some progressions

> Some progressions cannot be played with a pick only; you must use your fingers

**Significance (p. 93).** Right-hand technique requirement.

## Accepted blues progressions

> Most of these progressions are among the accepted ones used by musicians who play blues.

**Significance (p. 93).** Frames the catalog as standard practice.

## Blues sound from dominant 7

> the blues sound is usually associated with the dominant 7th family.

**Significance (p. 93).** Identifies dominant 7 family as the blues color.

## I in bar 1, IV in bar 5

> in 99.9999% of all cases, some form of [ chord will be in bar lto
> start the progression, and some form of a IV chord will be in bar 5.

**Significance (p. 93).** Defines the structural skeleton of the 12-bar.

## 9th chords for bluesier sound

> This progression has a much bluesier sound to most ears due to the abundance of 9th chords

**Significance (p. 95).** Explains color-via-extensions for blues.

## Understand-why principle

> Do anything that sounds good to you, but try to understand why you are doing it.

**Significance (p. 93).** Methodological principle: ears + analysis together.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
